name: Fish (Sardines, Anchovies, and other oily fish)

book_claims.claim: Acceptable animal protein source in a longevity-focused diet when consumed moderately
book_claims.mechanism: Provides essential amino acids and omega-3 fatty acids without the same negative metabolic profile as red meat

book_claims.claim: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids that reduce inflammation
book_claims.mechanism: Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids activate anti-inflammatory pathways (resolvins, protectins)

book_claims.claim: Replacing meat with fish contributes to lower cardiovascular mortality
book_claims.mechanism: Reduces saturated fat and L-carnitine/choline intake while increasing omega-3 fatty acids, lowering TMAO production and inflammation

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis of 38 prospective studies (Jayedi et al., 2021) found fish consumption of 2-3 servings/week associated with 12% lower all-cause mortality and 15% lower cardiovascular mortality compared to no fish intake.

VITAL trial ancillary study found marine omega-3 supplementation (1g/day) reduced total cardiovascular events by 28% in participants with low baseline fish intake, supporting the importance of dietary fish (Manson et al., 2019).

Examine rates fish/fish oil as having strong evidence for triglyceride reduction and moderate evidence for cardiovascular risk reduction, anti-inflammatory effects, and improved cognitive function in older adults.

taste: mild to strong depending on species

culinary_pairings.source: Combined unsaturated fats explaining low heart disease in Crete despite 40% calories from fat
Classic acid complement; vitamin C aids iron absorption
Traditional preparation for sardines and anchovies
Lycopene + omega-3 for combined anti-inflammatory effect
Quercetin-rich complement; traditional sardine and anchovy pairing

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Marine omega-3 directly usable by the body, unlike plant ALA which requires conversion. Sardines: 1,100-1,600mg; anchovies: 2,300-2,400mg per 100g.
One of few significant dietary sources of vitamin D3; sardines and salmon among the richest.
Higher bioavailability than red meat; fish consumption associated with lower colon cancer risk unlike red meat.

synergies.description: Combined unsaturated fat sources that together explain why Crete had low heart disease despite 40% calories from fat
Marine omega-3 (EPA/DHA) from fish complements plant-based omega-3 (ALA) from walnuts for comprehensive anti-inflammatory coverage
